description: Mastering the total tax increase and the 2026-2027 cycle. Use PASSWORD 'tax2026'.

questions.q: The total tax increase reflected on 2026 property tax bills is 7.7%.
questions.info: Correct! While the municipal part is 6.9%, the provincial education portion brings the total to 7.7%.

questions.q: A citizen's total bill increase is exactly 6.9% regardless of school tax.
questions.info: False! 6.9% is only the municipal portion. The total impact is 7.7%.

questions.q: The total 7.7% increase includes the provincial education tax requirement.
questions.info: Correct! The education tax is set by the Province and collected by the City.

questions.q: The 2026 property tax payment deadline is June 30, 2026.
questions.info: Correct! This remains the firm deadline for the full balance.

questions.q: A 2% service fee applies to 'catch-up' amounts for mid-year MPP enrollment.
questions.info: Correct! This is a one-time fee applied when enrolling after January.

questions.q: The City keeps the entire 7.7% increase for municipal services.
questions.info: False! Part of the 7.7% total is for the Provincial Education fund.

questions.q: Property assessments for the 2026 cycle are based on July 1, 2025 values.
questions.info: Correct! Market value is always 'frozen' as of the previous July.

questions.q: If a caller says 'taxes went up 7.7%', they are referring to the total bill increase.
questions.info: Correct! 7.7% is the figure that accounts for all portions of the bill.

questions.q: Waste collection rates increased by 7.7% to match the property tax hike.
questions.info: False! Waste collection rates actually decreased by approximately 3%.

questions.q: The MPP installment amounts update in June to reflect the new tax rates.
questions.info: Correct! This accounts for the 7.7% total increase for the remainder of the year.

questions.q: Late penalties (starting July 1) are calculated on the full unpaid balance.
questions.info: Correct! Ensure payments are received or postmarked by June 30.

questions.q: 311 agents can waive the 7.7% increase for seniors on low income.
questions.info: False! Agents cannot waive taxes, but can refer seniors to the provincial deferral program.

questions.q: The total 7.7% increase was finalized during the Spring 2026 budget adjustment.
questions.info: Correct! Council finalized these rates on April 17, 2026.

questions.q: If a caller sold their house on June 1, they are not responsible for any taxes.
questions.info: False! Taxes are usually adjusted between lawyers, but the current owner is responsible to the City.

questions.q: You can pay property taxes with a credit card at the Edmonton Service Centre.
questions.info: False! Credit cards are only accepted via third-party online providers with a fee.

questions.q: The 7.7% total increase is mandatory and cannot be appealed.
questions.info: Correct! You can appeal your assessment (value), but not the tax rate itself.

questions.q: Monthly MPP withdrawals occur only on the 1st of every month.
questions.info: False! Customers can choose the 1st or the 15th.

questions.q: If June 30 falls on a Sunday, the deadline is Monday, July 1.
questions.info: Correct! The deadline moves to the next business day.

questions.q: Edmonton's school tax portion is set by the City Council.
questions.info: False! School tax is set by the Alberta Government, not City Council.

questions.q: A Change of Address can be completed by a 311 agent over the phone.
questions.info: Correct! Agents can update mailing addresses, but not legal ownership names.

questions.q: A 'Supplementary Tax' bill is only sent if a property is sold.
questions.info: False! It is sent when new construction or renovations are completed mid-year.

questions.q: The 6.9% municipal increase is only one component of the 7.7% total bill increase.
questions.info: Correct! Agents should be clear that the 7.7% is what the citizen actually pays.

questions.q: MPP allows citizens to pay their 7.7% increase over 12 interest-free months.
questions.info: Correct! This is the primary benefit of the Tax Payment Plan.

questions.q: The deadline to appeal a 2026 Assessment was March 23, 2026.
questions.info: Correct! By the time tax bills arrive, the assessment appeal window is closed.

questions.q: 311 agents can provide tax balance information to a caller's Realtor without consent.
questions.info: False! This requires owner authorization or a formal Tax Search certificate.

questions.q: If a bank account has insufficient funds for a MPP withdrawal, the City will try again the next day.
questions.info: False! The City does not re-try. A $25 service fee is applied and the citizen must pay manually.

questions.q: MPP enrollment is automatically cancelled after two missed payments in a year.
questions.info: Correct! The account will be removed from MPP and all remaining taxes become due June 30.

questions.q: Property owners must notify the City in writing to cancel their MPP at least two weeks before the next payment.
questions.info: Correct! The deadline is the 15th of the month to stop the next month's payment.

questions.q: Local Improvement Charges are included in the 7.7% general tax increase.
questions.info: False! Local Improvements (like alley paving) are specific to certain properties and are additional.

questions.q: The City offers a discount for paying property taxes early in the year.
questions.info: False! Edmonton does not offer early payment discounts.

questions.q: Citizens can choose to split their school tax between Public and Separate boards.
questions.info: False! School support must be 100% to one board or the other, as per the School Act.

questions.q: Title ownership changes are updated in the City system immediately after a sale.
questions.info: False! It can take 4-8 weeks for Alberta Land Titles to process and notify the City.

questions.q: The City can grant a tax exemption for a property because it is currently vacant.
questions.info: False! Vacancy is not a valid reason for property tax exemption under the MGA.

questions.q: New homeowners are not responsible for late penalties if the previous owner didn't pay.
questions.info: False! Penalties stay with the property; the new owner is responsible for the full balance.

questions.q: A $25 service fee is charged for any dishonoured (NSF) MPP payment.
questions.info: Correct! This fee is consistent with the City's User Fee Bylaw.

questions.q: Property owners can view and print their 2026 tax notice via MyProperty.edmonton.ca.
questions.info: Correct! Owners can access their digital notices using the access code from their bill.

questions.q: The MPP program is only available to residential property owners.
questions.info: False! MPP is available for residential, multi-family, and commercial properties.

questions.q: You can register for a MPP account change (like a new bank) by calling 311.
questions.info: False! Banking changes require a signed form and a void cheque/direct deposit form.

questions.q: The Provincial Education Tax is calculated based on the same assessed value as municipal taxes.
questions.info: Correct! Both portions use the frozen July 1 market value from the previous year.

questions.q: Edmonton property taxes can be paid at most Canada Post outlets.
questions.info: False! They can be paid at banks, online, or the Service Centre, but not Canada Post.

questions.q: The City's 'Tax Estimator' tool allows citizens to see the impact of the 7.7% increase.
questions.info: Correct! Citizens can enter their new assessment value to see an estimated total.

questions.q: If an owner disagrees with the 7.7% increase, they can file a complaint with the Assessment Review Board.
questions.info: False! The board only hears complaints regarding assessment values, not tax rates or increases.

questions.q: The tax year in Edmonton is the same as the calendar year (January to December).
questions.info: Correct! Property taxes cover the full calendar year.

questions.q: An 'Amended Tax Bill' is issued if an assessment appeal is successful.
questions.info: Correct! If the value is lowered after a hearing, the City issues a new bill with the lower amount.

questions.q: The City can put a 'tax notification' on a property title if taxes are 1 year in arrears.
questions.info: Correct! This is the first step in the tax recovery process.
